sentences.text: Well, the lady let us know.
They let us know that we had to-- we had to leave, that the-- the lager is going to be liquidated.
And we had to leave.
First left the men, and then they left the women.
We had to leave.
But we-- we thought we are going to Brunnlitz!
You know?
Oskar Schindler wanted to have 300 women.
I was one of the 300.
And-- and we-- we thought we going to Brunnlitz!
And in the meantime, our train-- in this cattle train, which we were in-- went to Auschwitz.
So we thought we are going-- this is gonna be the end.
We thought we are gonna be gassed there-- killed there, yes?
Was your husband already in Auschwitz, at that time?
My-- my Auschwitz-- my husband didn't go to Auschwitz.
My husband went to Brunnlitz there.
And then, from Brunnlitz, he-- he-- he took him to Gross-Rosen.
He couldn't stay there.
He had to leave there because-- because of the child.
I left to Auschwitz.
We are-- we had to go to the-- in-- to the-- to the gas chamber-- to-- not the gas chamber-- to the shower.
A matter of fact, when I saw the movie, and I saw the-- the film-- the scene in the-- in the-- in the shower, where the women-- you remember?
Did you see the film?
I was looking for myself.
Because we went through exactly the same thing.
We were standing there in the shower when we was looking-- is there going to come water, or is there going to come gas?
We didn't know-- exactly the same.
When I-- I was looking.
I was saying, what is-- that-- that must be me!
So we went there.
And then we-- we went to a block.
We were to got-- we got clothing.
Clothing-- they-- they made it funny.
For-- for a short-- short woman, they gave long dresses.
For a tall woman, they gave short dresses.
We looked like-- they cut our hair.
They didn't shave me.
They cut it, you know, very short.
But some of the girls they shaved completely.
There was snow.
There was ice.
That was in November-- in November, 1944.
First, yeah.
They-- they-- they-- there came some woman, and [NON-ENGLISH], a German soldiers woman.
And they-- they told us where to go.
Sure.
Oh, we got-- we got a visit of Mengele.
We got-- we are ordered to get naked on the-- on the appellplatz, and he looked us over.
Sure, sure.
I'll tell you something.
In this time, our group of people were happy to be-- women happened to be young, healthy, beautiful women.
It was the-- the prime of-- or was-- was it? 19-- 1944.
I'm born 1907.
I was 34 years old, yes?
No.
No comments, no.
No.
No job-- nothing-- just waiting-- waiting to be killed.
But then came a angel.
Then came Oskar.
And he took us out from there.
Four weeks.
Four weeks.
Four weeks of torture.
Because you didn't know.
You didn't-- from one moment to the other, you didn't know what's gonna happen!
You didn't know.
You want-- do you want-- we didn't do any work.
You know?
That made us very worried, because when you got tattooed and you got-- we didn't get tattooed, even.
You know?
So we were worried.
Every minute-- every minute of day or night, you are worried.
What's going to happen?
During the day, did they-- they didn't-- they didn't have you come outside, or--
It was just to the appellplatz to be counted.
Three times or sometimes two times-- three times a day, we had to walk to the appellplatz.
It was cold.
We were naked.
We were-- nothing to wear.
We was sticky.
And they counted us that we are so and so many, and black in the-- back in the block.
Yeah, they gave us some coffee, bread-- not enough, of course.
On-- you know, on britches, on a stool.
Yeah.
Horrible.
Horrible.
Just horrible.
Just-- and-- I don't believe it either.
I think it-- it must have been a dream.
I couldn't have come through that.
Then-- then they called us by name.
Each of us they called by name.
And they took us outside, and they put us on a train.
Yes.
And then we have found out from the-- there was a German soldier.
I saw my son there!
Yes.
We got in a-- in a-- in a-- in a train.
And there came-- there was a hole in the train-- a hole in the wall.
And my sister-in-law, she looked through this little hole.
And she wanted to see-- there were no windows.
There were cattle train, for-- for cows, or horses, or whatever.
And she looked through this little hole.
And she saw some little boys.
And so she looked.
And she looked-- over the wire.
And she said, Manci, Manci, come here.
Look.
I think this is Alex-- this is Alex-- Alex and Richard here, so.
And that was Alex and Richard, and another little boy, [NON-ENGLISH].
And-- and in-- in this moment, there comes a soldier in-- a German soldier.
And he says, is here Horowitz, Regina, and Marianne-- Manci Rosner here?
He says, yes.
And he gave us a letter.
He was the one who-- who took my husband and my brother-in-law from Brunnlitz to Gross-Rosen.
And he told them that he's going to go now to Auschwitz and bring women to Brunnlitz.
So they gave us a note.
And he took the note.
He was a nice, nice, good German man.
And then I begged him.
I said, please.
Because when we had to go to do something, we went under the train, you know?
So I said, please, would you let me go under the train?
There is my son, and I would like to whistle to him or make myself known to that boy that I am here!
So he gave us the permission.
We went under the train.
You know, under the train it was about this high where the wheels are.
And I went under the train.
And I whistled-- we had a certain whistle.
And he-- he-- I spoke to him, [NON-ENGLISH], I saw him there.
How are you, [NON-ENGLISH], How are you?
Are you hungry?
He said, no.
No, I am not hungry, mama, I have potatoes.
And he showed me he had some potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the last time I saw him till-- till after the liberation, in Munchen.
No, he-- we left together with my husband.
Husband, all the time.
Yeah.
No, I-- I thought I meet them there, but I didn't meet them.
They had to leave there, because there couldn't be children.
He had to leave there.
And they took away his violin.
And then in Gross-Rosen, there was also a very, very big coincidence.
When they took away his violin, he said, this is not my violin.
This violin belongs to Mr.
Schindler.
He took it away just the same.
And when he came to Brunnlitz, and so-- Oskar ask him, Henry, where is your violin?
He said, they took it away from me.
So he said, why didn't you say that it belonged-- he said, I said that.
And he got it back.
He got it.
He gave it to me.
Yeah.
Yes.
Then we left.
The train left.
They closed the doors.
The train left, and we went to Brunnlitz.
Oskar Schindler wanted us.
We were the-- the specialized workers which he needed in his factory.
And he made a list of everybody that he wanted.
Everybody, by name-- 1,000 men and 300 women-- or 800 men.
I think it was 1,100 people all together.
Of course!
Of course.
Of course, of course-- just the only reason.
And he didn't know me.
I never saw him before.
I met him in-- in Brunnlitz.
If you know that when we-- once we are in Brunnlitz, that we are safe.
He was there.
He was standing there.
With the quarters were nothing terrible-- just britches too, but we know that we are not going to be killed there.
We're not gonna be gassed there.
We are not going to be beaten.
This we know.
Yeah, he said, yes.
He said, I'm glad you are here, and I wanted you to be sure that I am going to stay with you till five minutes after 12:00.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm gonna stay with you, and we are gonna get it through.
He had the ammunition factory.
I worked on a-- on a machine.
We made some ammunitions-- which supposed to fit in-- in-- the thing never fitted.
Not one ever fitted.
And he bought it-- he bought it in the black market, the ammunition which he delivered.
That's right.
That's right.
No.
No.
When I was there!
No, we didn't know.
We didn't know nothing.
We knew only that he is not a murderer.
He's not a murderer.
And he's not a-- and-- and we heard that he said to the Germans there that there is gonna be no beating.
There is gonna be no killing, that these are workers.
They going to work.
And--
Sure.
We had woman guards-- German woman soldiers-- guarding, and not-- over here.
Sure, there were guards.
When we had to go to the toilet, we had to toilet-- to the latrine.
And we had to have a guard.
We couldn't go by ourselves.
When he was in Brunnlitz.
Sometimes he had to go on business.
He had to travel, you know?
Then he wasn't there.
No.
No.
He was-- he just came there.
He-- he gave us a-- he smoked, and he-- he has a-- cigarettes came in packages, in little boxes-- hundreds.
So he had this package, and he made one-- one he-- one thing, and he threw it on the floor.
He take another one, on the tools, and we could pick up the cigarettes.
Oskar?
Oh, we adored him.
He was, for us-- he was-- he was-- I think he was sent down from-- from God as an angel.
I think so.
Because that what he did, nobody else would ever done.
I think so.
I think so.
And God bless Spielberg-- what he did for this film.
I did-- we all did it.
I-- I myself-- the film and this, also-- we did it.
I did it only because I wanted Oskar Schindler's name should be known forever.
I didn't do it for myself.
I don't need-- I don't the-- the thing here.
I only did it because I only want my children, my grandchildren, my great-grandchildren, they should know that Oskar Schindler existed.
Yeah.
None whatsoever.
He was in Gross-Rosen.
And from there-- this I found out later-- they sent him to Dachau.
He's-- he was the eighth-- the last till-- all this time what I was in Brunnlitz, he was in Dachau with Alex-- with my-- with my boy.
They had typhus.
They were sick.
The-- the-- they supposed to be gassed.
They're supposed to go to the gas chambers there.
So there was one woman soldier which recognized him-- that he was playing for Goth.
So they took him out of the gas chambers.
Yes.
Oh, he said, you-- you-- they wanted to take Alex only to the gas chambers.
My son said, when you take him, you take me, too.
They went with him together.
Then they went out-- came out.
She recognized him-- that this is the violinist.
He's a-- a musician.
There to the end of the war.
He was liberated by the Third Army-- American army-- by General Eisenhower.
Yeah.
We were liberated by the Russians.
Which one?
Brunnlitz.
B-R-U-- with two-- N-L-I-T-Z.
Yeah, we are-- we are have-- we had a radio.
We had a radio.
And we heard that the-- the Russian is coming nearer, and nearer, and nearer.
And we heard-- we heard the bombing already, you know?
And-- and then we have-- we have-- we heard Winston Churchill's speech in the-- in the factory.
Oskar Schindler had the loudspeaker from the radio.
And we heard that the Germans surrendered.
We heard it.
And nothing!
I-- next morning, we got up, there were no Germans.
They all run away.
We all on our own.
Nobody was there.
Nothing!
You know, we-- we were-- we were walking around.
We didn't know what to do!
They run away!
They all run away.
All the Germans run away.
Then what happened-- the Russian came on horses.
They came in, and they-- they brought us food.
They feeded us, and then they let us out.
And each of the-- each of us, we went to apartments where the Germans run away.
And we-- I was a dressmaker, so I took down some curtains, and I made some skirts for the girls.
And we-- we spent there a few days.
And then we went-- I had a date with my husband.
When we live through the war, we're gonna meet in Krakow.
So my-- there was with me was my brother-in-law, my niece, brother-- two brothers, and a sister, and one sister-in-law.
And I said, we go to Krakow.
So we went.
There were no trains.
The-- the-- the-- the tracks were bombed.
So we walked for a while.
And then we saw a horse with a platform-- you know-- this thing.
So we stole.
We stole this horse on the platform, and we went with this horse.
We sit on the platform.
We went with this horse, with this wagon, to-- till we finally came to some little town where was already a train.
So we left the horse on the platform.
We went on the train.
We told to the-- what-- who we are.
And on the way there we stopped somewheres.
And we said again we was.
So then they took us to a school-- Red Cross, like, you know?
And they gave us something to eat.
And we went-- it took us about maybe a week.
You know, till we are-- till we got to Krakow.
Let me take you back a little.
Then we'll pick up over there.
The day that-- that you were liberated by the Russians, was Oskar Schindler there?
Yes, he--
Yes, said-- he said, do you know you are-- you are-- you are free now.
You can do-- and he-- now we had to save him.
We had to save him, because he was in danger.
As a German, when somebody would have-- so he put on-- they got a car for him.
His car was there.
And one-- a few-- a few of our people-- they-- he put on the-- the-- the-- this-- the suit for the camp-- the striped suit.
And they-- they went.
They wanted to take him to the Swiss border, you know, to save him.
And they got him somewheres.
They-- they saved him.
They saved him.
Very emotional.
Very emotional.
We wanted to give him something to remember.
So some was-- one of these-- one of the-- our haftling-- our prisoner-- he had some golden teeth.
So they took out, with-- with-- with regular thing-- they took out these golden teeth from him.
And one of them was a-- a jeweler.
And he made him a ring, for this gold.
He made him a ring, and he engraved in this ring, 'Whoever saves one person saves the world.' And they gave him this ring.
Yeah, all of us were there.
He was crying.
He was-- he broke down.
Yeah.
And they took him away.
Though, they saved him.
They-- he-- he was-- and we got-- everybody got on their way.
One went to Krakow.
One went to Warsaw.
One went to-- wherever they go-- the-- they think that they can.
In an apartment.
Yeah, there was a beautiful sewing machine-- a Pfaff sewing machine.
I had a ball.
I sewed all these things.
I made some brassieres, you know?
And for-- for skirts, kerchiefs, because we didn't had no hair, you know?
So there were curtains, bedspreads-- whatever I could cut off there, I will.
And from there, I know you told us that you got-- that-- took the horse-- stole the horse.
We went.
We went.
We wanted to go to the-- there was no train, because the tracks were-- this section was bombed.
It couldn't be no train.
On the way-- on the way in--
In Krakow, we are-- I went back to-- to-- to this-- when I was before, when the war broke on, I was in a furnished room with my husband and with my child.
And I went back to this lady, which-- so I said to her, look.
I am here.
I have no money.
But would you give me this one room?
I had two rooms in this thing.
Give me one room.
And you know I am a dressmaker.
I'm gonna make some money.
I'm gonna pay you.
And she gave it to me.
And I stayed in this room there.
And then-- then a friend of mine moved in with me.
She was also a dressmaker.
And we started to sew-- you know, we started to make a-- earn ourself a living so we could go to a bath, you know, we could take care of ourselves a little bit.
And then, after maybe that we were liberated in May-- that must have been in-- in-- we were there till September, I think.
In September, there came a-- a newspaper in Krakow.
From-- there was an article-- a one page-- regards from prisoners-- not from prisoners-- it was from ex-prisoners from Dachau to their families in Poland.
And, between all those, there were, Henry Rosner and his son.
So this is how I find out that he is alive.
So I had to go, but I didn't know how to-- how to communicate!
There was no telephone.
There was no mail.
There was no way of communicating.
So I said to my girlfriend, [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
I'm gonna go on the way.
I'm gonna try to go to Dachau and see what's happened.
So she said-- she said to me, I go with you. she had-- she her husband, [NON-ENGLISH].
So we-- we got on the way.
We got-- took us about two weeks.
There were no trains, you know?
Partly there were trains, partly there were no trains, partly we are-- we are-- we schmoozed.
We talked with some English soldiers.
They take us somewheres.
Then we got-- we found some Americans.
They took us.
We got there.
We got to Munchen.
Munchen-- and there we found-- I found my husband-- my husband and my son.
Oh, this-- this I cannot tell you.
This is-- this is something which is undescribable-- undescribable.
I have no words to tell you that.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
So this was in September-- September 1945.
And my husband-- while he was in Dachau, on the end, when the American army came in, he befriended with one [NON-ENGLISH], major, Dr.
Morse.
He was a dentist by profession.
And he was an amateur cello player.
And he wanted to play.
And there was another prisoner, a Polish guy-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
He was a piano player.
And my husband was the violinist.
And they found a violin for him there.
And they played together.
And they got to talking.
So Henry said that he has a brother in America which went there in 1939, to the Polish-- to the World-- World Fair in-- in-- in New York.
But he doesn't know his address.
So Dr.
Morse says to him, what-- he's-- he's a pianist.
He's a-- so the father of Dr.
Morse was also a musician, and he belonged to the musicians' union in New York.
So he said, I'm gonna write to my father, and my father is going to find the address of George Rosner in New York.
And that's how it was.
And that's how we contacted my husband's brother, in New York.
And he sended us affidavits.
And in-- in May-- on the 24th of May, 1946, we are landed in New York.
